content.assessment: This proposal engages precisely with the centrally organised admissions process (F-live-hungarian_education_system-03), attempting institutional reform at the level where the 85%-selective-school statistic is generated (F-live-hungarian_education_system-01). Institutional fit is comparatively strong because it works within the existing Ministry-managed process rather than replacing the structure. However, system coverage is structurally limited: church-maintained and private schools operate under separate exemptions (F-live-hungarian_education_system-05) and fall outside standard admissions authority, meaning a Klebelsberg Centre-administered reform cannot reach a segment of the segregation-producing sector. This creates an unintended sorting risk in which reform-compliant public schools become more accessible while exempt institutions remain untouched or even absorb displaced selective demand.
opportunities: Precedent exists for admission-level interventions producing measurable segregation reductions without structural abolition
strengths: Works within existing centralized admissions infrastructure rather than requiring new governance architecture
Directly targets the SES-correlated selection mechanism documented as an OECD outlier
threats: Non-covered exempt schools could function as an unintended sorting valve, concentrating advantaged demand outside the reformed admissions channel
weaknesses: Church-maintained and private schools' admissions exemptions place a substantial share of the sector outside the reform's institutional reach
Administrative compatibility is undermined by fragmented authority over admissions across maintainer types
